sentences.text: She was not so happy.
But she sees she can't talk to me.
So then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she followed me, but we don't talk much.
And then I go to-- my father was so upset that I'm there.
But when I arrived, about two minutes, again, the bell ring.
And that man come again.
And he said, they changed their mind.
They take the girls away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I went under the bed, to nobody should see me.
And when he went, I said to my father, look, if I go back now, they shot me straight away.
Because I was a [NON-ENGLISH].
Escape.
So what you want what I should do?
If I go back, that's the end of it.
Because the transport is gone.
So what they do with me?
Shot me.
So, then he go to conference again with his wife to the other room.
And he come back.
And he said, you know, stay.
We will think something about it.
You know, we-- we need time to-- to think about it.
And that's what do.
That's what we do all afternoon.
That was already about 5 o'clock in the afternoon already.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we talk about it.
And he said, you know, I have a friend.
He has a friend, a school friend, that priest.
Dr.
Klobetz, Sandor.
They was in the school together.
And my father always help him.
Because my father was a generous man with everybody.
Not with his family, but-- not with me.
But to the other people, he was always a gentlemen.
And he always help him.
And he said, I think him, and I talk to him.
So he ring him.
And he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think about it.
Give me a few hours.
And then the night come.
And I can't sleep in his place, because you never know when they come to see what's happening.
So my grandmother, his-- his mother's house was about half a corner from his house.
And a non-Jewish girl lived there.
It was a beautiful home.
And they give it to her.
And she looked after it.
And she lived there.
And they said when they come home, they will see what's happening.
So my father have a key.
And they took me there when it was dark.
And [INAUDIBLE] to room where is the housekeeper used to sleep.
So they put me there.
And tomorrow morning, they said I will talk to that [NON-ENGLISH].
The priest.
Priest, and then we will see what's coming.
And that's how was it.
The next morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father sent somebody.
I don't know, who was it?
I think it was his wife who is go to the-- to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH].
How you--
Town hall.
And I have a good friend of mine there, a girl who was with me together from the kindergarten.
We grew up together.
But she don't live that town anymore.
She went to [INAUDIBLE] in Czechoslovakia.
Her father was an [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] officer of the air force.
And she went studying in [INAUDIBLE].
So she was not living in Ujpest.
So we take her papers out.
Because I know when she born, her auntie name, her grandmother.
Because we was very close together.
So we get the papers.
You had no trouble getting the papers?
No.
Because they-- they don't know she's not in that town anymore.
And they give it to her.
I think my step-mother.
She went there.
[INAUDIBLE]
No, it's in Hungary.
The Bible, Bible.
Biblical name.
And we was-- how I say to you.
Together-- we went to the high school together.
We went together.
Only when I go to the technical college, she went to-- she come to be a-- not a nurse.
It's a-- in the countryside, you, you know, they look after the sick people.
Yeah.
No.
Yes.
Yes.
Birth certificate, everything.
I only needed birth certificate.
That's-- that's all what I need, because that's the everything.
And it was the same age, and how I know where the aunt is, and the grandmother, the grandfather name.
I-- I know everybody.
And the priest had a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] holiday place, actually, in Balaton.
It's a grapes--
A vineyard, yeah.
And he has a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] housekeeper.
And the housekeeper's mother look after his property over there.
So she was an elderly woman who lived alone.
So she was happy if I go to her and help her to the winery and things and the chickens, and the moo cows, and everything.
And I went there.
How did you get there?
The first time in my life-- I never-- never was that side of the country.
I go with the train.
And when-- when the train is gone, then it's an air things come again.
Air raid.
And they put us out again, because that was the rule.
Have to leave the-- the wagon, or the--
Carriage, you have to leave.
So we're running down to the field.
And I was hoping it's coming a moment, killing me, you know?
I was so unhappy.
And I don't know why I live, you know?
Only one thing was to see my mother again.
But was hopeless, because now-- then I heard that they went in different way.
How can I get over there?
Because now they push me that side of the-- of the country.
And she went that side of country.
Did you think she was still alive at the stage?
Yes, because nobody see her coming out of wagon-- of the wagon.
They see her going in.
And she started to call me and my brother, because she was alone with my uncle and my auntie and my cousin.
And-- and she calling my name.
But nobody see coming off the train in the wagon.
Yeah.
Because I was looking for her, you know, to find her.
That was-- even if I can't find her, but at least to know where is she-- where is she.
Yeah.
My brother was in the [NON-ENGLISH] camp with his friend.
They-- because they don't look Jewish.
My brother was blonde, and curly, and blue eyes, like my grandfather used to, my mother always said.
And they escaped.
And they find him-- find them.
And one of his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] friend come back said, front of them, they shot him.
The Germans.
No, they-- they tried to escape.
The tried to escape.
And they got-- got them and bring them back.
In front of the other boys, they shot him.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
He was only 22.
Yes.
Yes.
And my name, too.
Because nobody escape in that transport who go from that factory.
Nobody.
Yes.
Nobody come home.
So the factory give the list to the [INAUDIBLE] people, for those girls are gone.
And when was the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
My memory-- the memory.
You know, that wall.
The memorial wall.
And I read my family name.
And that was my name there, too.
Of course, yes.
They don't know I escaped.
Nobody knows.
They think I was in  the wagon with the others.
They all went to Auschwitz.
All-- my-- my grandmother was 72 like me now.
And I have auntie.
And I have a cousin.
And--
Once he told me-- once, I don't know what he mean by then.
But he said to me, if once more your mother coming back, it will be a different life.
I don't know.
Maybe he-- he wanted to change his life.
Or-- but he was only 48.
My mother was 46.
And he was 48 when they kill him.
Yes, Balatonakali.
Yeah.
Yes.
I was there about till September.
I do everything.
And by-- by hand, I make some frocks for the village girls.
And they bring me some presents.
So I have a lovely life, because everybody likes me.
And I sing with them.
And I milk the cows.
Like.
And you lived with the housekeeper's--
Mother, yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
Yeah, they still have the-- they still have the--
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
But they say to them I am the priest's niece.
So they give me a covet, because I was somebody.
But I still mixed with them, so they-- they think I am so generous, too.
No.
No, because, you know, the priest have a little boy cousin who used to be there in the summer holiday.
And-- but this year, he went in a camp with the school.
So he can't come.
So that's why I was there.
That's how they understand.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
And the non-Jewish people, they have a ball of their life.
Because all the Jewish money, and the house, and the jewelry and everything, they live like queens and kings.
You know?
It's not their money and not their house.
They're just sitting in the beautiful homes to the Jewish people.
They was happy.
No, but when I come-- I asked the priest-- I wanted to go back to the city, you know?
And he was a very sick man.
He has asthma, a very sick man.
And he need always somebody around him to look after him with the medicine.
And that's what I do to him when he was with us in that summer place in Balaton.
And of course, he has a little chapel over there.
And she-- he always pray every morning, make a mass.
And I help him.
He teach me what to do, how to do.
I know everything.
And--
Of course.
Yes.
Because he wants I should become a Catholic, of course.
No.
He don't say.
But I have a feeling.
Because that's why he teach me, isn't it?
If he don't want I should become one of his people, yeah, then he wouldn't do that, yeah?
And he loves me-- he liked me.
Because I was a clever little girl, and I do everything to please him.
And I learn.
I learn a lot.
He teach me how to dress them up to go to the mass.
And to give them the wine and prepare the things.
I was the little boy-- you know this.
Yeah, but I was alter girl.
So-- and every morning, I prayed with him.
And-- and-- because maybe-- because my grandfather was a Catholic, how I told you.
Maybe I was not a [INAUDIBLE] Jew, you know?
I was a between them, you know?
Half, half way.
And--
I enjoy it, yes.
Because I always like to know everything.
And-- and it was something new for me, you know?
I never-- I never learned that.
And I was fascinated, you know?
And-- and-- because he was so good to me, I'd do anything to him to please him.
And I know he like that I should learn that, so I learned for him.
So you-- so you helped him in the chapel?
Of course, yes.
And I cleaned the chapel.
I put the fresh flowers there, you know?
And he was so proud of me.
And when-- when he took me back to his place in the city, unfortunately, the-- the man who was working-- to the-- to the bellman, he has a bomb kill him, and was nobody to help.
So I went there.
But he teach me in his-- in the vineyard, in the little chapel.
So I know everything.
So I went every morning and help them to the priests to put on dress and the [INAUDIBLE].
This is when you went back to Rakospalota.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yes.
And when we-- I come with the-- with the-- I call him uncle, you know.
And when we go to the station, then I help him.
Because he was so sick and weak, I hold him in his hand and his arm.
And then somebody said, stop that Jewish girl.
And for a moment, I think they're calling me.
And he hold my hand.
He said, don't move.
So we walk slowly and slowly.
And we don't move the head.
And next to me, a Jewish girl was there.
And somebody recognized her.
And they came and take her away.
How did you feel?
Can you imagine how I feel?
I was so shaky.
And I don't know what to do.
Because-- he said, just calm down, calm down, calm down, you know?
And we walk so slowly.
I think he help me, not I help him that time.
Because I was so upset and so shocked to see the other girl is gone.
Everywhere, of course.
Everywhere and every-- and they walk around the-- the--
Yes.
Everywhere, of course.
And they stop, and-- don't need to-- can I have your papers?
Can I have your papers, you know?
Everywhere, because was many--
Was Germans and Hungarians everywhere.
I think the Hungarians was worst.
I think so.
Because they go to their head.
They think they are somebody now.
They-- all [INAUDIBLE] those nobody's who that time was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because if somebody was somebody, they don't do that.
Of course.
Of course they were cruel.
Just to Jews.
And those ones who-- who sympathizing with the Jews, of course.
They was rude to them, too.
Of-- look, they take my father away.
And my father was so sure of, always, of himself.
So he smacked the-- the--
--guard, the [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
And when they take them to shot them in the [INAUDIBLE]--
--only-- only only he-- his hand was strangled together and the feet.
Because he would escape.
And he want to kill everybody.
And they shot him.
His wife come to me to the priest's house.
Because that was already in November.
November.
Yeah.
This next day, she come to me and told me.
They come early in the morning.
Because they know he has all the-- the jewelry things for-- for everybody, for my family, and his family, you know.
Because only he was the only one.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
Yes, he was suspicious.
But he don't believe it.
He was thinking that nothing will happen to him, because Mr.
Jeno Spitzer, you know?
Nothing would happen to him.
But he was wrong.
They came 4 o'clock in the morning.
And they took him away.
They-- they put everything upside down.
And my father told my little brother who was 10 at that time-- my half brother-- he said, in [NON-ENGLISH], in the--
Pantry.
They put the jewelry there.
If anything happened to them, he should know.
And when they come, the little boy panicked and run straight away to the pantry.
And they take everything away.
Everything.
They take to the-- to the-- [NON-ENGLISH] house.
And then they interview him.
And next morning, they take him to the [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
And shot him, 10 of them.
Yeah.
She come to--
I don't know.
I don't know.
Probably she don't come to me.
I think she-- she go to the [NON-ENGLISH].
To his friend, yes.
Because he-- she knows he liked him.
They was very close when even was young boys.
They grew up together, and they went to school together.
And I think she come to him, not me.
But I was there.
Yeah.
I think it's-- now it's-- everything what is not good then, now it's finished.
So I think I-- I am finished.
But that uncle, that priest was so nice to me.
He said to me, you're my little girl, now.
I am, you know, your father's friend.
So-- and long as I live, I look after you.
I don't let anything happen to you.
No.
No.
Your father is gone.
The killed him.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was not a-- not a-- soft people, you know?
She was a-- in my eyes-- maybe, maybe because of me.
But probably if my stepbrother heard that-- that one, you know, he will be upset.
But, you know, when my brother write me a letter and come to Australia and I help him to come here-- because like my mother teach me, it's not his fault.
He's your brother.
And he was about 20, 22 at that time.
And I said to him, you want your mother to come out after you?
He said, yes, please.
And I don't like her.
I-- I never like her.
But I said to my husband, I said, listen darling, that's his mother.
My mother teach me to like him, so I think we should try to bring her out.
To Australia.
And I was sure if he-- if she come, she make miserable my life again, you know, even to see her.
Not to live with her, but to see her.
But I said to my darling husband.
I said, but we have to do it for him.
So we fixed it up the papers and everything.
We got a deed.
And we sent the ticket to the boat and everything.
And then she get a cancer.
And the day when she should come out, she died.
So the God-- I believe God.
And I said, she don't-- the God wanted to I should make the sacrifice to show Him what type of person I am.
So I bring her out.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he don't let her come to make my life miserable.
I believe that.
So after she told you the news of your father, did she go back to her house?
Yes.
Yes.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the war, she was fighting off that-- that property.
She take her children from her first marriage to my father's house-- not me.
